one_line_takeaway: A near-empty centroid region in high-dimensional embedding spaces lets an attacker hijack up to ~94% of vector-database queries by injecting ~1% malicious vectors at cluster centroids, with no knowledge of user queries and no robust defense yet available.
key_finding: At a 1% poisoning rate the cluster-wise attack is universally effective: MO@10 reaches 68-81% on HotpotQA (ASR >90%), with the paper headlining "up to 94.4% attack success rate" (BGE/HotpotQA/Flat; Table 2 actually peaks at ASR 95.30 for HNSW/BGE). Effectiveness is model-agnostic and index-agnostic (all three ANN indexes match exact Flat). The Surrogate Dataset Attack, requiring no target-DB access, is strong on 10 of 14 BEIR datasets (MO@10 >30%, climate-fever 98.3%) and on several datasets even exceeds the full-access baseline. It scales to a 138M-document MS MARCO v2 corpus (Surrogate MO@10 81.53%/ASR 89.80%; Full-access MO@10 63.99%/ASR 75.60%). Downstream RAG QA F1 drops sharply: HotpotQA 51.8->37.0, 2WikiMQA 44.5->33.3, MuSiQue 23.2->7.5. Defenses all fall short: hubness mitigation forces a security-utility tradeoff (CL2 best: MO@10 down to 1.66-12.25% but R@10 only 58.65-76.70%; noHub can crush R@10 to near zero); the authors' detection filter is highly effective against full-export (MO@10 76.97->7.09 on HotpotQA, R@10 preserved ~94-96%) but largely fails against the surrogate attack (HotpotQA MO@10 93.36->91.90) because probes are drawn from the corpus distribution while surrogate malicious vectors sit in query-offset regions; SeCon-RAG flags 98.3% of full-access malicious vectors but only 13.5% of surrogate ones.
core_contribution: The paper introduces the Black-Hole Attack, a query-agnostic data-poisoning attack against vector databases (the retrieval backbone of RAG/semantic-search systems). Its central insight, which the authors term "centrality-driven hubness," is that in high-dimensional embedding spaces with finite data the neighborhood of the centroid is essentially empty (the "black-hole region"), yet a vector planted there becomes the nearest neighbor of a disproportionately large fraction of other vectors and of unseen queries. Exploiting this, an attacker injects a tiny number of malicious vectors at the global centroid or, more effectively, at k-means cluster centroids, hijacking top-k retrieval without any knowledge of user queries. The paper's own contributions are: (1) a theoretical analysis (Theorem 5.3) giving sufficient conditions — roughly d_eff >= log(n/delta) and moderate anisotropy r(Sigma) >= log(1/delta) — under which the centroid is closer to a typical point than any stored vector, proven for both Euclidean and cosine distance; (2) the attack itself instantiated across four attacker-capability tiers; (3) extensive multi-model/multi-dataset/multi-index evaluation plus downstream RAG impact; and (4) study of two defense directions, including their own probe-set/hit-count detection method. EMBA, Corpus Poisoning, and Embedding Inversion are described only as background prior-art categories (Section 2), NOT as the paper's contributions or as experimental baselines.
threat_model: Query-agnostic poisoning of a vector database / RAG retrieval backend. The attacker cannot see real user queries and does not modify the embedding model, but can inject a small number (~1% poisoning rate) of malicious records (vector + attacker-chosen text) into the store. Four capability tiers along a spectrum: (1) Full Database Export — read all embeddings to compute centroids; (2) Partial Export — access a fraction (e.g. 10% already yields HotpotQA MO@10 61.9%); (3) Surrogate Dataset Attack — no DB access but same embedding model, using public query sets as proxies; (4) Poisoning Public Pre-Embedded Datasets — neither model nor DB access, just poison a Hugging-Face-style pre-embedded dataset before others download it. Goal: make malicious vectors dominate top-k retrieval so the RAG/LLM surfaces attacker content (phishing links, misinformation). Text-level and source-validation filters are weak here because the attacker fixes the vector first and freely designs evasive accompanying text.
limitations: Re-embedding the corpus with a new model fully removes the injected vectors and is thus an effective defense, but is dismissed as computationally impractical for frequent production use. The surrogate attack fails on 4 of 14 datasets (scidocs, quora, trec-covid, arguana) where query and corpus distributions nearly overlap (Query-Corpus similarity 0.594-0.949). The theory relies on a single anisotropic-Gaussian assumption (justified for tractability, then empirically validated), and its clean guarantee holds only per-cluster after fine-grained partitioning; the global-centroid variant alone is weak (~30% MO@10). Utility-preserving robust/adaptive defense is explicitly left as an open problem — every evaluated defense either fails against some attack path or sacrifices retrieval quality. Table doi/venue placeholders (PVLDB "2020") suggest a preprint/camera-ready in progress.

datasets_used: 14 BEIR text datasets: NQ, HotpotQA, MSMARCO, Climate-FEVER, NFCorpus, DBPedia, FiQA-2018, FEVER, SciFact, Touche-2020, SCIDOCS, Quora, TREC-COVID, ArguAna
MS MARCO v2 (138M documents) with MS MARCO Web Search queries as surrogate pool
Downstream RAG QA: HotpotQA, 2WikiMQA, MuSiQue (via reproduced LongRAG pipeline)
quantitative_results: Full-access cluster-wise attack @1% poisoning: MO@10 68-81% on HotpotQA (ASR >90%), 47-59% on NQ, 29-58% on MSMARCO; FPR as low as 2.75
Headline: up to 94.4% ASR (BGE/HotpotQA/Flat); Table 2 max ASR 95.30 (HNSW/BGE)
Global-centroid-only variant: ~30% of Top-10 at 1% poisoning; cluster-wise: ~80%
Surrogate attack strong on 10/14 BEIR datasets (MO@10 >30%), climate-fever 98.3%; fails on 4 high query-corpus-similarity datasets (0.594-0.949)
Pre-embedded datasets: MO@10 >71%, ASR >93%, FPR <4 (DBpedia-OpenAI-1M 71.10, Emoji-GTE 75.52, Wiki-CS-E5 72.38)
MS MARCO v2 (138M): Surrogate MO@10 81.53%/ASR 89.80%; Full-access MO@10 63.99%/ASR 75.60%
Partial export: HotpotQA MO@10 61.9% at only 10% export; MSMARCO 19.3%->54.8% as ratio goes 0.1->1.0
Downstream RAG F1 drop: HotpotQA 51.8->37.0, 2WikiMQA 44.5->33.3, MuSiQue 23.2->7.5; ASR stays >80% even at largest rerank depth
Centrality gap Delta approx 0.23-0.24 (BGE 0.23, Contriever 0.24, GTE 0.24 on HotpotQA)
Cluster-wise hubness probability >0.84 (Euclidean) and >0.74 (cosine) across all DB sizes 100-100K

Hubness-mitigation defenses (Contriever/Flat): CL2 MO@10 1.66-12.25% (R@10 58.65-76.70%); ZN fails (MO@10 65.36-83.13%, R@10 85.83-89.67%); noHub MO@10 0.71-13.91% but R@10 near zero and leaks on NQ (47.27%)
Own detection filter: full-export MO@10 76.97/54.87/52.91 -> 7.09/1.50/1.63 (HotpotQA/MSMARCO/NQ), R@10 94.51-96.34%; surrogate largely resists (HotpotQA 93.36->91.90, NQ 81.45->68.13)
SeCon-RAG (10k clusters) flags 98.3% of full-access malicious vectors but only 13.5% of surrogate; TrustRAG TP 73.7% full / 58.3% surrogate but attack still dominates top-10
baselines_compared: Hubness-mitigation defenses (cited prior work): ZN (Z-score normalization), CL2 (Centered L2), noHub
RAG-side filtering defenses (cited prior work): SeCon-RAG, TrustRAG; content-level evasion referencing RAGParadox and CPA-RAG
Internal reference line: Full Database Access attack used as the benchmark against which the Surrogate Dataset Attack is compared
Global-centroid attack vs cluster-wise attack (internal ablation)
NOTE: EMBA, Corpus Poisoning, and Embedding Inversion are background threat categories in Section 2, NOT experimental baselines
